summary: The post argues that the analytics community has built a reputation for being welcoming, but that welcome is not shared equally, with Black professionals profoundly underrepresented in conferences and the field. The author contends that diversity should be pursued as a moral imperative, not a business case, and that the community’s blind spots risk embedding and amplifying racism through data-driven systems.
money_quotes: This shows the limits of self-perceived kindness and progressivism.
They turn systemic racism into systematic racism, encoded and executed automatically, relentlessly, at scale.
A culture of congeniality can be just as ruthlessly biased as a toxic one, while also being better disguising its closed doors.

Unless the community becomes more visibly diverse, “data literacy” could become one of racism’s most powerful professional variants—and the analytics community could, even inadvertently, be one of its most forceful accelerants.
The data industry has the money to do more. It remains to be seen if it has the commitment.
key_insight: A community can be outwardly kind yet structurally exclusionary, and in data this exclusion risks hard-coding racial bias into systems and careers.
